Step 5: PBO/PAI Coding. Simple screen: 2 days. Complex screen with table control and validations: 4-5 days. Yeh developer experience pe heavily depend karta hai.

Step 6: GUI Status in SE41. Ek standard status with common buttons: 3-4 hours. Multiple statuses (different per screen): 1 day.

Step 7: Testing. Development effort ka 40% testing ke liye allocate karo — unit testing, integration testing, negative testing. Table control wale screens ko extra testing time chahiye.

Step 8: Bug Fixing. Testing mein bugs milenge — estimation mein 20% development time bug fixing ke liye include karo.

Step 9: Documentation. Test script + tech spec update: 1-2 days.

Step 10: Buffer. Unknowns ke liye 20-25% extra always add karo — integration issues, unclear requirements, performance tuning.

Estimation Template: 1-screen simple = 7-8 days. 1-screen with table control = 10-12 days. 3-screen complex = 25-35 days. This is a practical guide, not exact science.
module-pool-007.interviewMeKyaBolnaHai:
Interview Me Bolna Hai:
'For Module Pool effort estimation, I break it down by component: screen design in SE51 takes 1 to 2 days per screen, PBO and PAI coding takes 2 to 3 days per screen, a table control adds 2 extra days, GUI status creation takes half a day, testing takes approximately 40 percent of development time, and documentation takes 1 to 2 days. I also add a 20 to 25 percent buffer for unknowns like integration issues and unclear requirements. For a typical single-screen Module Pool with no table control, the estimate is around 7 to 8 days. I adjust upward for junior developers and for complex multi-screen workflows.'

Hinglish tip: 'Estimation mein 40% testing ka rule bolna shows maturity — beginner developers sirf coding time estimate karte hain aur testing ko bhool jaate hain. Interviewer yeh sun ke samajhta hai ki tum real projects pe kaam kar chuke ho jahan testing bhi timeline mein hoti hai. Buffer percentage mention karna bhi professional approach dikhata hai.'
module-pool-007.codeExamples:
" EFFORT ESTIMATION TEMPLATE (as structured comments)
" Requirement: Custom Vendor Approval Module Pool
" Estimator  : Developer Name | Date: 2025-01-15

" TASK BREAKDOWN:
" Requirements analysis  : 1.0 day
" Technical design (TDD) : 1.5 days
" SHDB recording (BDC)   : 0.5 day
" Screen 0100 design(SE51): 1.0 day  (header, 12 fields)
" Screen 0200 design(SE51): 2.0 days (table control TC_ITEMS)
" GUI Status (SE41)       : 0.5 day
" PBO/PAI coding - S0100  : 2.5 days
" PBO/PAI coding - S0200  : 4.0 days (table control)
" Unit testing            : 4.0 days (40% of coding)
" Bug fixes               : 2.0 days (20% of coding)
" Documentation           : 1.5 days
" Buffer (25%)            : 5.0 days

" TOTAL ESTIMATE: ~25 days
" Developer level: Senior (reduce 30% for senior+)
" Junior developer: add 35-40% = ~35 days

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you estimate effort for screen design in SE51?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: SE51 screen design ka time depend karta hai screen complexity pe. Simple screen — text fields, dropdowns, checkboxes, 10-15 fields: 1 day. Medium screen — 20-25 fields, multiple groups, some field attribute logic: 1.5 days. Complex screen — table control, multiple containers, many conditional fields, field symbols: 2-3 days. Factors jo time increase karte hain: table control (add 1 extra day just for TC design), dynamic field attributes (LOOP AT SCREEN logic), custom layouts with many groups, responsive design for different screen sizes, popup dialogs (CALL SCREEN).
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Screen design effort in SE51 depends on complexity. A simple input screen with 10 to 15 standard fields takes about 1 day including field placement, attribute settings, and flow logic connections. A screen with a table control adds 1 additional day for the control definition, column setup, and scroll area configuration. If the screen requires dynamic field activation using LOOP AT SCREEN based on user roles or modes, that adds another 0.5 to 1 day. I always add 20 percent to SE51 estimates for unexpected layout adjustments during review.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Screen design mein sabse zyada time layout iterations mein jaata hai — functional consultant ne screen dekha, bola 'yeh field wahan hona chahiye, yeh group alag karo'. Ek complex screen ke liye 2-3 layout review rounds normal hai. Estimation mein yeh revision cycles include karo.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Sirf initial design time estimate karna, revision cycles ignore karna. Screen design mein functional consultant ke saath 2-3 review sessions hone chahiye — har session mein changes hoti hain. First design almost kabhi final nahi hoti.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you estimate PBO and PAI coding effort for a Module Pool screen?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: PBO coding time: simple — SET PF-STATUS, SET TITLEBAR, default values, basic auth check: 1 day. Complex — table control LOOP, dynamic field attributes, multiple conditions: 2 days. PAI coding time: simple OKCODE handling with 3-4 cases and basic validation: 1 day. Complex validation (cross-field, DB lookups, multiple save operations): 3 days. Table control PAI (LOOP AT WITH CONTROL, MODIFY, DELETE): extra 1.5 days. Multi-screen navigation logic: extra 0.5 day per screen transition. Database save logic (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on multiple tables): 1-2 days depending on tables.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: PBO coding for a standard screen — setting GUI status, title, default values, and basic field attributes — takes about 1 day. PAI coding for a screen with 4 to 5 OKCODEs including save, back, and validation takes 1.5 to 2 days. Complex PAI with table control loop handling, row deletion logic, and multi-table database operations extends to 4 to 5 days. I always estimate PAI at roughly 1.5 times the PBO estimate since PAI holds the majority of business logic. Shared utility function modules — validation, save, fetch — can reduce PAI coding time if reused across screens.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Ek project mein 3 screens tha — each screen ka PAI almost similar validation logic tha. Pehle teen alag PAI module likhey with duplicated code — 9 days total. Next project mein common validation function module banaya reusable — 6 days total saving. Reuse strategy estimation mein consider karo.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Error handling time estimate mein include nahi karna. Happy path code karna 2 days mein ho jaata hai, but error scenarios handle karna, MESSAGE statements, rollback logic — yeh sab double the time ho sakta hai. Estimation mein error handling time explicitly add karo.

followupAnswerBank.question: How much effort should you allocate for testing a Module Pool program?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Testing ka golden rule: development effort ka 40% testing ke liye allocate karo. 10 days development = 4 days testing. Yeh kaise distribute karo: Unit testing (developer khud karta hai) — 50% of test effort. Functional testing (developer + functional consultant) — 30% of test effort. UAT (business user) — 20% of test effort. Table control wali screens ko extra 1-2 days testing time chahiye — edge cases bahut hote hain. Regression testing when there are changes — separate estimate karo per change request, minimum 2 days regression. Performance testing — agar busy screen hai (50+ concurrent users expected) — extra 1 day load scenario testing.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: I allocate 40 percent of total development effort for testing, distributed across unit testing by the developer, functional testing with the consultant, and UAT with business users. Screens with table controls get additional testing time — at least 2 extra days — to cover edge cases with zero rows, bulk rows, row deletion, and scrolling. Regression testing after any code change is estimated separately at a minimum of 2 days. For high-volume screens with expected concurrent usage, I include 1 day for performance scenario testing and reviewing ATC findings.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Project manager ne estimation mein testing time cut kiya — '40% testing zyada hai, 20% karo.' Developer ne accept kiya. UAT mein 15 bugs nikle — jo proper testing mein pehle hi aa jaate. Bug fixing + re-testing mein 40% se zyada time lag gaya. Total project late hua. Moral: testing cut karna typically later mein zyada time leta hai, save nahi karta.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Testing effort ko development effort ka sirf 10-15% estimate karna — yeh unrealistic hai. Real module pool programs mein validations, navigation, table controls sab test karne mein time lagta hai. 40% is an industry-accepted baseline for SAP dialog programs.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:

" Testing Allocation: 40% = 6 days

"   - Navigation flow                : 0.5 days

" Functional Testing (Dev + FC)      : 1.5 days
"   - Happy path end-to-end          : 0.5 days
"   - Negative test cases            : 0.5 days
"   - Edge cases (0 rows, max rows)  : 0.5 days

" UAT Support                        : 1.0 day
"   - Attend UAT sessions            : 0.5 days
"   - Bug analysis and clarification : 0.5 days

" Performance Test                   : 0.5 days
"   - ATC review, SELECT in PAI check
" TOTAL TESTING: 6.0 days (40% of 15)

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you estimate effort for a Module Pool with SHDB recording integration?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: SHDB (Screen-based recording) Module Pool ko BDC program mein automate karne ke liye use hoti hai. SHDB recording ka effort: simple single-screen recording — 2 to 3 hours. Multi-screen recording with complex flow — 0.5 to 1 day. SHDB recording ke baad BDC program likhna: basic CALL TRANSACTION program — 1 day. Complex program with error handling (BDCMSGCOLL), error report, re-processing logic — 2 to 3 days. SHDB recording ke baad test data prepare karna: 0.5 to 1 day. SHDB + Module Pool combination ka total additional effort: 3 to 5 days for full BDC integration.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: When a Module Pool program also requires SHDB recording for BDC integration — for data migration or batch processing — I add 3 to 5 days to the base estimate. This covers: 0.5 day for SHDB recording in transaction SHDB, 1 to 2 days for the BDC calling program with CALL TRANSACTION using lt_bdcdata, 1 day for error handling using BDCMSGCOLL with error recording and reprocessing, and 0.5 day for test data preparation and BDC run verification in modes A, E, and N.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Data migration project mein 10,000 records module pool se process karne the. SHDB recording 1 day, BDC program 3 days (error handling complex tha), testing with 100-record test file 1 day, full load testing 1 day — total 6 days sirf BDC integration ke liye. Base Module Pool development alag tha. Estimation mein yeh clearly separate karo.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: SHDB recording ko 'quick 30 minute task' samajhna. Recording karna fast hai, but recorded script mein edge cases, dynamic field values, conditional screens handle karna complex hota hai. Especially jab module pool screens mein dynamic OKCODEs hain ya conditional navigation hai — BDC recording accurately capture karna mushkil hota hai.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" SHDB + BDC Integration Effort Estimate:
" Task                          | Days
" SHDB Recording (SHDB tx)      | 0.5
" BDC Calling Program - Basic   | 1.0
" Error Handling (BDCMSGCOLL)   | 1.0
" Error Log Report              | 0.5
" Reprocessing logic            | 0.5
" Test data preparation         | 0.5
" BDC testing (modes A/E/N)     | 0.5
" TOTAL BDC ADDITION            | 4.5 days

" BDC calling code snippet:
CALL TRANSACTION 'ZDEMO_MP'
  USING lt_bdcdata
  MODE gv_mode        " 'A'=test, 'E'=errors, 'N'=prod
  MESSAGES INTO lt_bdcmsg.

IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  " Error handling — log for reprocessing
  APPEND ls_error TO lt_error_log.
ENDIF.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you handle estimation for unknowns and buffer in Module Pool projects?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Unknown risks ke liye buffer mandatory hai — koi bhi project 100% defined nahi hota pehle se. Module Pool specific unknowns: 1) Requirement unclear hain — functional spec mein 'as needed' jaisi vague lines hain — discussion time aur rework. 2) Integration dependency — module pool ko koi existing FM ya BAPI call karna hai jo expected behavior se alag kaam kare. 3) Performance issues — heavy screen pe production load simulate karna testing phase mein. 4) Retrofit — customer ne UAT mein new requirement add kiya. Buffer add karna professional practice hai — 20% for medium-risk, 25-30% for high-risk or unclear requirements.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: I always add a buffer to Module Pool estimates to account for unknowns. For well-defined requirements with clear functional specs, I add 20 percent. For requirements that are partially defined or involve integration with existing programs or legacy data, I add 25 to 30 percent. Specific risk factors I account for: time for clarification meetings when specs are ambiguous, integration issues with existing function modules or BAPIs, layout revision cycles with functional consultants, and scope changes during UAT. I explicitly call out the buffer in the estimate so project managers understand it is not padding but risk mitigation.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Estimate 15 days diya tha without buffer. Client ne 3 change requests diye during development — field name changes, extra validation, layout changes. 15 days se 22 days ho gaya. Deadline miss. Next project mein 20% buffer add kiya in estimate — 18 days. Client usi change requests ke saath 20 days mein deliver kiya, 2 days early. Buffer ek professional practice hai, not laziness.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Buffer ko specifically estimate mein show nahi karna — bolte hain '15 days' but internally 12 days sochte hain. Agar project manager actual buffer jaane woh accordingly plan kare. Transparent estimation builds trust — explain karo ki buffer kisliye hai (risks X, Y, Z ke liye).
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" BUFFER CALCULATION TEMPLATE:
" Base Development Estimate: 20 days

" Risk Factors Identified:
" 1. Functional spec incomplete (3 open questions) -> +10%
" 2. Integration with existing Z-FM (untested)      -> +5%
" 3. Table control first time for this developer    -> +5%
" 4. Unknown data volume for performance testing    -> +5%
" Total Risk                                        -> +25%

" Buffer: 20 days * 25% = 5 days
" Adjusted Estimate: 25 days

" Estimate Breakdown for Project Manager:
" Development (design + code)  : 15 days
" Testing (40% of dev)         :  6 days
" Documentation                :  2 days
" Sub-total                    : 23 days
" Risk buffer (25%)            :  5 days
" TOTAL ESTIMATE               : 28 days
" (rounded from 23+5=28)

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you estimate differently for a junior versus senior developer in Module Pool?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Developer experience ka estimation pe directly impact padta hai. Senior developer (5+ years Module Pool experience): base estimate use karo — SE51 1 day, PBO/PAI 2-3 days per screen. Mid-level developer (2-4 years): base estimate pe 25-30% add karo. Junior developer (0-1 year): base estimate pe 50-60% add karo, plus 10-15% mentor review time (senior developer ke review sessions). Junior ke liye additionally: first time SE51 use karna — extra training time 0.5 day. Table controls for first time — extra 1.5 days learning curve. Debugging skill — junior debugger mein slow hote hain — extra testing time.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Estimation must account for developer experience. My base estimates assume a senior developer with 4 or more years of Module Pool experience. For a mid-level developer, I add 25 to 30 percent. For a junior developer, I add 50 percent plus allocate 15 percent of the total estimate for senior developer code review and knowledge transfer time. Junior developers also need additional time for their first encounter with table controls — approximately 1.5 extra days — and their debugging sessions are slower, requiring more testing time. I always discuss developer assignment with the project manager before finalizing estimates.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Project mein junior developer ko assign kiya Module Pool ka. Estimation 10 days di thi senior ke hisaab se. Junior ne 18 days liye aur quality issues the — senior ne 4 days review aur rework mein daale. Total 22 days. Agar pehle se junior factor kiya hota — 15 days estimate + 5 days review = 20 days, realistic tha. Better planning, less surprise.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Project manager ko bataye bina assume karna ki 'junior hi karenge, same time mein ho jaayega.' Estimation mein developer level explicitly mention karo: 'Yeh estimate assumes Senior ABAP developer with Module Pool experience. Adjust accordingly for other experience levels.'

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you estimate effort for documentation in a Module Pool project?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Documentation effort Module Pool project mein often underestimated hota hai. Types of documentation: 1) Technical Design Document (TDD) — 1 to 2 days (initial creation). 2) TDD updates per change request — 0.5 day per CR. 3) Test script document (all test cases) — 1 day. 4) User manual / training document (agar required) — 2 to 3 days. 5) Transport request documentation — 0.5 day. Total typical documentation effort for a 2-3 screen module pool: 3 to 5 days. Yeh roughly 15-20% of total project effort hota hai. Many projects skip this, tab future maintenance costs 3x ho jaati hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Documentation effort for a Module Pool project is typically 15 to 20 percent of total development effort. For a 2 to 3 screen program, this means 3 to 5 days covering: 1 to 2 days for the initial technical design document, 1 day for the test script with positive, negative, and boundary test cases, 0.5 day per change request for TDD updates, and 0.5 day for the transport object checklist and release notes. If a user training document is required, that adds 2 to 3 days. I always include documentation effort in my estimates explicitly — leaving it implicit means it gets dropped under schedule pressure.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Audit mein auditor ne pucha: 'Custom transaction ZDEMO_MP kaisa kaam karta hai, kaise maintain karte hain, authorization kaise manage hoti hai?' Agar TDD nahi hota toh source code hi dekhna padta — auditor acceptable nahi manta. TDD tha toh 20 minute mein audit query resolve ho gayi. ROI of documentation clearly seen.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Documentation ke liye zero time estimate karna, bolna 'code mein comments hain, kafi hai.' Code comments != Technical Design Document. TDD structured information provide karta hai jo code comments nahi dete — screen inventory, OKCODE mapping, transport checklist, auth checks — yeh sab comments mein nahi hote.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you present effort estimates to project managers and clients?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Estimate presentation mein important hai ki sirf ek number nahi dena — breakdown dena. Project manager aur client dono ko transparent estimate chahiye. Best approach: 3-tier estimate dena — Best Case (sab perfectly clear hai), Most Likely (normal assumptions), Worst Case (unknowns materialize hote hain). Example: Best Case 15 days, Most Likely 20 days, Worst Case 28 days. Breakdown table dikhaao: development, testing, documentation, buffer alag alag. Assumptions clearly list karo — 'Yeh estimate assume karta hai ki functional spec complete hai, senior developer available hai, no scope changes.' Agar assumption galat nikla toh estimate change hoga — yeh communication pehle karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: I present Module Pool estimates as a breakdown table with separate line items for design, coding, testing, documentation, and buffer — never as a single number. I use three-point estimation: Best Case assuming everything is clear and senior developer is available, Most Likely for normal project conditions, and Worst Case if key assumptions do not hold. I explicitly state the assumptions underpinning the estimate — complete functional spec, specific developer experience level, no scope changes during development. This transparency enables project managers to make informed planning decisions and prevents the common problem of estimates being taken as commitments without understanding the underlying assumptions.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Client ne 8-day estimate maanga tha single-screen module pool ke liye. Breakdown diya: development 5 days, testing 2 days, documentation 1 day = 8 days BUT with assumptions: spec is complete, no table control needed, senior developer. Client ne 3 changes maange during development — each change estimate se discuss kiya. No surprise at end. Professional relationship maintained.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Pressure mein low estimate dena sirf client ko khush karne ke liye — phir overtime karna, quality compromise karna, ya late delivery. Yeh short-term mein khushi aur long-term mein trust damage hai. Honest estimate with proper breakdown aur assumptions builds long-term credibility.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" THREE-POINT ESTIMATION TEMPLATE:
" Requirement: 2-screen Module Pool, Customer Entry

" BEST CASE (spec complete, senior dev, no changes)
" Screen Design     : 2.0 days
" PBO/PAI Coding    : 4.0 days
" Testing           : 2.5 days (40% of dev)
" Documentation     : 1.0 day
" BEST CASE TOTAL   : 9.5 days (~10 days)

" MOST LIKELY (normal project conditions)
" Above + 25% buffer = 10 * 1.25 = 12.5 days
" MOST LIKELY TOTAL : 13 days

" WORST CASE (unclear spec, mid-level dev, 2 CRs)
" Most Likely + dev level adj (25%) + CR estimate (2*2d)
" WORST CASE TOTAL  : 13 * 1.25 + 4 = ~20 days

" ASSUMPTIONS:
" 1. Functional spec approved and complete
" 2. Senior ABAP developer assigned
" 3. Dev system with required master data available
" 4. No table control required (add 3 days if needed)
" 5. No scope changes after design approval
